---
title: "Disaggregated Inference"
date: "2026-07-05"
categories: [LLM, inference, disaggregated inference, systems]
draft: true
---

Disaggregated Inference (DI), also known as disaggregated serving, disaggregated prefill, P/D disaggregation, is an LLM serving architecture that separates the prefill and decode phases of inference onto different hardware resources. Compared to other LLM inference technique disaggregated inference is pretty new and trendy paradigm. As this is moving area, in this post I will cover the higher level conceptual view of DI,  consideration of its usage of DI in production system, and recent trend as I see it.

### Quick Basic and Motivation

I think basic is well known that LLM inference phase has distinct requirement in its two phases of execution, prefill and decode. Still a couple lines to recap this concept. The prefill phase happens when LLM process all the input tokens together, it is compute heavy and relay on dense matrix-matrix multiplication. The modern GPUs kernels have been gradually improved over the last few years to tackle the compute challange of prefill phase. However, the decode phase when LLM generates one token at a time it become extremely memory bound, because for every tokens LLM needs to read all weights from the memory, and this phase it very low compute as it relay on matrix-vector multiplication.

Another noteworthy point is that inference provider and enterprise are extremely cautious about the token cost, to reduce the cost of tokens high-throughput serving is required but that hurts interactivity or individual user's experience in terms of the token/sec they receive. The pareto curve shows that higher the interactively demands lower batch size and that increase the cost. So industry is deeply trying to find the way to improve the interactivity (token/sec/user) keeping the lower cost or keeping higher throughput.

The primary benefit of disaggregated serving is improving the interactivity. When both the phases of the LLM are colocated on the same GPU, the decode phase can suffers from the prefill phase of the incoming new request, as continous batching allows new requests to always fill-in the stream. So the each requests in the decode batch can get interrupted (called "Head-of-Line" Blocking), as a result ITL (inter-token latency) can be irregular and interactivity suffers. By disaggregation these decode phase can happen in its own decode GPU pool, hence a steady-state, stable ITL is achived improving throughput.

With the primary benefit stated above, there are additional benefit comes in the disaggregated setup, such as:

1. Independent optimization and scaling: As prefill workers and decode workers are separated they can often scaling  independently to maximize the throuhgput based on the varying workload. Independent parallelism, batching, and P:D ratio flexibilty.
2. Disaggregation provides even more flexibility where different types of accelerator can be used which might be better fit for compute bound vs memory-bandwidth bound work.

#### Architectural consideration of traditional GPU-GPU disaggregated inference

With the basics covered, let's see what are the main component of the disaggregated serving. Here it is worth noting that though disaggregated inference can be done within a node (for example 4GPU for prefill, 4 GPU for decode), I will mainly discuss about disaggregated inference in the cluster.

In the distributed inference system, there are traditionally two main components, router, which works in the request/data plane between the requests to the servers, and autoscalar which works in the control plane to scale the servers. In the disaggregated inference we have further granularity, prefill clusters are dedicated prefill servers to run the prefill workload, decode clusters are decode servers to run the decode workload, and kv connector or transfer engines, which are data plane movers from the prefill servers to the deocde servers.

**Dynamic xPyD**

Disaggregated inference scaling impose scaling requirement of prefill and decode clusters. Prefill heavy workloads such as summarization tasks, big document processing can cause lesser number of request processing in the prefill servers compared to the decode servers causing bottleneck limitted by number of prefill servers, even if decode servers can be underutilized. On the other hand, decode heavy workload such as long reasoning tasks, decode servers can become bottleneck. Hence a static scaling such as 1:1 ratio of prefill and decode often leads hardware underutilization and suboptimal throughput. Hence the distributed inference framework such as NVIDIA Dynamo (SLA based planner) or llm-d (WVA, Workload Variant Autoscaler) watches signals like prefill queue depth and decode-side KV cache usage, SLO targets, and scales the prefill and decode servers accordingly.

**Co-scheduling consideration in disaggregated inference**

Disaggregated inference introduces a **co-scheduling problem** as prefill and decode workers are separate but tightly coupled, so their placement, scaling, and routing all need to be coordinated together rather than treated independently.

1. **Physical Proximity** -- Prefill and decode workers need to be scheduled in close physical proximity to each other (same rack, zone, etc.) so that KV cache transfers between them are fast and low-latency. This can be handelded by topology aware gang scheduling.
2. **KV Cache Transfer Efficiency** -- The scheduling system needs to be aware of the KV cache transfer cost and **route requests to decode workers that are topologically close to the selected prefill worker**, maximizing cache transfer performance.
3. **Balancing the ratio of prefill vs. decode workers** --  The scheduler needs to independently scale prefill and decode worker counts based on live traffic and SLA targets (TTFT and TPOT). For example, it can skip disaggregation and fallback to colocated inference for small load,

#### Fallback to Collocation

One important caveats to remember, disaggregation does not always worth it for each request. An intelligent router can asses that and can route the request entirely on the decode server where it can do both prefill and decode and generates all the token. Under these conditions it can happen

1. If the prefill length of a request is short it can be efficiently computed in the decode engine by piggybacking chunked prefill requests with ongoing decode requests eliminating the need of small KV transfer overhead
2. Prefix cache hit: Prefill becomes memory bound when prefix cache hit occurs in the decode servers. and hence can be more efficiently computed in the decode engine.
3. The the prefill queue is overloaded with long queue, it could be better to serve it in the decode servers until more workers are allocated on the prefill queue.

#### The transport layer: KV Cache movement

The KV Cache transfer from the prefill clusters to decode clusters is critical, a slow transfer path wipe out benefit of disaggregation. Inference engines such as VLLM or SgLang or distributed inference framework such as Dynamo or llm-d all tend to use one or more high-performance data transfer libraries such as Nvidia's NIXL , AMD's MoRI IO etc. These efficient libraries avoids CPU staging (copying from source GPU HBM to host RAM over PCIe before transferring to the destination GPU), and use asynchronous transfer over the ultra-fast interconnect fabric to make it as efficient as possible.

In Dynamo, the decode-side worker reserves KV cache blocks in its GPU memory and passes corresponding memory descriptor to the prefill worker. The prefill worker then uses NIXL transfer library, over RDMA-capable networking such as InfiniBand or RoCE, to access those decode-side GPU KV blocks directly. It may read existing prefix-hit KV blocks from the decode GPU, compute the missing KV cache, and write the newly computed KV blocks into the decode GPU's reserved KV memory. These KV blocks read or writes completely asynchronous so that both prefill and decode worker can cater other request when they are in-flight.

MoRI-IO is AMD's native transfer library optimized for KV-cache movement in disaggregated inference integrated into inference engines such as VLLM and SGLang. In vLLM, it is exposed through the MoRI-IO connector. It provides an optimized path to move KV cache between prefill and decode workers, with support for both decode-pull READ mode and prefill-push WRITE mode. WRITE mode is the default and usually faster because the decode side is prepared early, and the prefill side can push KV blocks as they are produced, instead of waiting for the full prefill to finish before starting the KV transfer. Depending on the deployment topology, MoRI-IO can use GPU-to-GPU transfer paths such as xGMI inside a node, or RDMA-capable networking across nodes.

For sparse-attention models such as **DeepSeek-V3.2 with DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA)**, KV access can become much more fine-grained because the decoder only needs selected KV entries. This makes **CXL-backed KV memory pooling** an interesting emerging direction, where KV can stay in a shared memory pool and be accessed on demand instead of relying only on RDMA-style KV transfer.

#### KV Cache management with disaggregated inference

In the disaggregared inference setup where many servers are running, reusing the KV cache accross all the servers in the efficient way become highly important. KV cache management layer such as LMCache turns KV cache from a temporary artifacts into reusable knowledge base that can be *stored* persistently and reused across multiple serving engines. It move KV caches out of GPU memory into a tiered storage hierarchy spanning CPU memory, local storage, and remote storage backends. This provides a **Decoupled Handoff** option Instead of a strict point-to-point transfer from prefill worker to decode worker. Also KV offloading from decode workers helps to maintain high-throughput. In today's agentic workload where massive documents and history or multiturn conversation the generated KV cache can be reused using this procedure, as prefill worker's compute can be avoided. Additionally Mooncake Store can provide a distributed backend for that cache, providing a shared KV cache pool that is facilitate by LMcache for KV cache management. The **high-performance remote storage engine** for KV cache: deciding how to store and retrieve it quickly and reliably.

#### Beyond traditional disaggregation

The disaggregation of prefill phase and decode phase opens the new opportunity to use entirely different accelerator for the decode phase which could be a better option for memory bound operation than GPU. Recently using SRAM centric accelerators are active research area for this. As we know GPUs needs to read all weights and KV cache from the off-chip HBM to generate one token at a time, which cause huge memory bottleneck. SRAM centric accelerator on the other hand are are good for memory bandwidth because irt reley on the fast on-chip SRAM to store the data. These accelerator does not have tired memory system so they avoid transfer delay from the off-chip memory to on-chip memory. As on-chip SRAM based accelerator typically have wider access path and distributed accross the chip, it provides a faster compute path to the compute units, provides a low-latency in the decode phase where GPUs are typically constrained by memory bandwidth bottleneck.

In this 2026, in first 6 months, I have observed multiple announcement and trends related to to heteregenous disaggregated inference. I feel these are really interesting trend and curious how all these solutions are spans out. I will quickly introduce some of them here

Nvidia announced AFD (Attention Feedforward Disaggregation) where decode phase's feedforward layers are accelerated with Groq's LPU. As we know every layer in the autogressive LLM consists of Attention followed by Feedforward layers. Attention parts need to read and write to growing KV cache, so that part stays on GPU, however after Attention, the feedforward path does not need KV cache, only needs output of the attention block accelerated on LPU, and this will continue in every layer.

A couple of more industry announcement

<https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/introducing-disaggregated-inference-on-aws-powered-by-llm-d/>

AWS announced

Speculative Decoding disaggregation: Speculative decoding is one of the  has a draft phase which is memory bound but the verification stage that is compute bound. This can also exploited by disaggregation.
